title: Type Modifiers 🚀
description: Tweak the size and sign of your variables.

slides.title: Stretching Limits 📏
slides.content:
A standard 4-byte `int` can max hold up to about 2.14 billion.

But what if you need to calculate the distance between galaxies, or you know a number will never be negative? C gives you **Type Modifiers** to stretch and tweak basic types.

slides.title: 1. Signed vs Unsigned 📉📈
slides.content:
By default, ints are `signed` (they can be negative or positive).

If you mark a variable as `unsigned`, it can **only be positive or zero**. By giving up the negative side, the positive maximum capacity immediately doubles!

`unsigned int players = 5000;`

quizData.question: What happens when you declare a variable as `unsigned`?

options.text: It requires twice as much memory.
It can no longer hold negative numbers.

quizData.explanation: Unsigned means the 'sign' (negative or positive) is removed, and all the available bits are used to store positive values only.

slides.title: 2. Short vs Long 📏
slides.content:
You can change how much memory an integer uses:

* **`short int`**: Usually 2 bytes. Useful for saving memory.
* **`long int`**: At least 4 bytes (often 8 on 64-bit systems).
* **`long long int`**: Always 8 bytes. Holds MASSIVE numbers.

You can even combine them! `unsigned long long int myWealth;`

quizData.question: If you are counting the exact number of stars in the observable universe, what data type modifier should you use?
quizData.explanation: The number is purely positive (unsigned) and extremely massive (long long).

slides.title: Format Specifiers for Modifiers
slides.content:
Different modifiers require different specifiers in printf:

* `%u`: unsigned int
* `%ld`: long int
* `%llu`: unsigned long long
